preamble: The voiced uvular fricative /ʁ/ can undergo complete deletion, resulting in its absence. This process is a strong form of lenition, often occurring in specific phonetic environments such as word-final positions or in casual speech. While often observed in rapid or informal speech, it can also be a feature of historical sound change or a common phenomenon in language acquisition reflecting tendencies in the adult language.

examples.note: Child speech example of 'girafe' (giraffe) in casual pronunciation, showing deletion of /ʁ/.
Child speech example of 'fromage' (cheese) in casual pronunciation, showing deletion of /ʁ/.

sources: Fougeron, C. (2010). Distribution and deletion of /ʁ/ in fluent speech. ProQuest Dissertations Publishing.
Rose, Y. & Demuth, K. (2008). The longitudinal development of clusters in French. Journal of Child Language, 35(3), 575-602.
Pépiot, E. & Fougeron, C. (2011). Perception and production of word-final /ʁ/ in French. ISCA Archive.
